postData.contentHtml:
New Zealand's right-wing coalition government is poised to unveil a 2024 budget described by some as a sharp acceleration of its assault on the working class. Set for formal release on May 28, the budget's announced measures indicate a clear prioritization: safeguarding the wealth of the super-rich and dramatically increasing military spending, reportedly in preparation for future conflicts.

Austerity Measures and Public Sector Cuts

Operational spending is slated for a meager 1.4% increase, less than half the current inflation rate, signaling significant cuts across public services. The government plans to eliminate approximately 8,700 government jobs over three years, a 14% reduction in staff. While healthcare workers, teachers, police, and military personnel are reportedly exempt from layoffs, the specific roles targeted for reduction remain unclear. This move is expected to exacerbate New Zealand's already rising unemployment rate.

Student Hardship and Soaring Debt

Young people face a major setback as the government confirms it will scrap the fee-free third year of study. This policy reversal will add up to $12,000 to the cost of a university degree. This comes at a time when student debt is already substantial, with over 618,000 students and former students owing a collective $16.1 billion. A recent University of Auckland survey highlighted the grim reality for many, finding 45% of students to be 'food insecure'. Further tightening the screws, 18- and 19-year-olds will be pushed off welfare benefits, despite youth unemployment sitting around 15%.

Attacks on Welfare and Housing Support

The budget also includes severe measures targeting the country's poorest. Rents for around 84,000 families and individuals in public or social housing will increase from 25% to 30% of their income, an average rise of $31 per week. While the Accommodation Supplement for low-income private renters will see a modest $15 weekly increase, almost 10,000 recipients, predominantly families with children, will lose their eligibility due to adjusted asset and income thresholds, resulting in an average loss of $42 per week. Additionally, the 'last resort' Temporary Additional Support (TAS) for the poorest welfare recipients will be cut, reducing the maximum benefit a person can receive. These cuts are projected to intensify New Zealand's severe social crisis, where one in five children lives in poverty, half a million rely on food banks, and 100,000 experience severe housing deprivation.

Massive Military Expansion

In stark contrast to the cuts affecting ordinary citizens, the government is funneling billions into the armed forces. A previous announcement outlined plans to double military spending from 1% to 2% of GDP over four years, amounting to over $12 billion. More recently, $1.6 billion has been allocated for naval upgrades and the acquisition of drones. Defence Minister Chris Penk emphasized the need for a 'combat capable New Zealand Defence Force', anticipating increased deployment in challenging international circumstances. Prime Minister Christopher Luxon has justified this militarization by citing the international situation, including China's expanding influence and the war in Ukraine, drawing parallels to World Wars I and II.

Political Backlash and Social Sentiment

The budget has ignited criticism from the Labour Party, the Greens, and union organizations, with protests planned for May 28. Labour leader Chris Hipkins has publicly opposed some cuts, although past Labour governments have also implemented austerity measures and supported military alliances. Despite the controversy, simulated social sentiment regarding the budget is described as 'mostly positive, some debate'.

This budget marks a pivotal moment for New Zealand, balancing fiscal conservatism with a significant pivot towards increased military engagement on the global stage, all while asking a substantial sacrifice from its most vulnerable citizens.
postData.title: New Zealand's 2024 Budget: Austerity for Workers and Students, Billions for the Military
postData.summary: New Zealand's upcoming 2024 budget signals a significant shift towards austerity, with deep cuts to public services, welfare, and student support, while simultaneously committing billions to military expansion. Critics argue this budget prioritizes the wealthy and defense over the needs of working-class citizens.

sources.title: New Zealand budget attacks workers and students, boosts the military
NZ trims new spending, sticks to surplus path as global risks heighten
